demographics: I am 55 years old. I am female. I am White. I am divorced. I had 6 years of college education (master's degree). My family income is $90,000 to $109,999. I am a strong Democrat. I am liberal. I attend religious services about once or twice a year.

events.event_text: I was laid off from my job at the publishing company where I had worked for nine years. It was a shock and a financial burden, but it also gave me the push I needed to start my own freelance editing business.
I found out I was pregnant during my senior year of college, a situation that wasn't planned but ultimately led me to adopt for the first time.

At 35, I took a stand in the workplace when I discovered our company was underpaying and overworking our interns. I organized my colleagues, and we successfully petitioned management to change their practices.

When I was 42, I decided to adopt again, this time a teenager waiting in the foster care system. It was a challenging process, but watching her grow and flourish in our home has been one of the most rewarding experiences of my life.

After years of advocating for better treatment of interns, I was shocked to be let go along with half of the workforce in a company-wide downsizing. It was a harsh reminder of how precarious life can be, but it also reignited my passion for advocating for worker's rights.

At 42, after years of advocating for fair treatment of interns, I adopted a teenager from the foster care system. For the first time, I felt I could truly make a difference not just in advocating for others, but in directly impacting someone's life.
At 38, I witnessed my company exploiting undocumented workers, paying them far below minimum wage. This shook me to my core, fueling my passion for workers' rights and immigration reform.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person was laid off from a long-term job, leading to both financial strain and the impetus to start a freelance business. They became pregnant during college, choosing to adopt and beginning a lifelong commitment to adoption. At 35, they advocated for fair treatment of interns, successfully changing company practices, and later adopted a teenager from the foster care system, finding it deeply rewarding.

This person, at 42, adopted a teenager from the foster care system, finding it challenging yet rewarding as the teen flourished. After years of advocating for fair treatment of interns, they were unexpectedly let go in a company-wide downsizing, reigniting their passion for workers' rights. Witnessing their company exploit undocumented workers at 38 further fueled their commitment to workers' rights and immigration reform.

persona_card: "Divorced, 55-year-old, liberal Democrat with a master's degree, she's a spiritual but not religiously devout freelancer who advocates for workers' rights and social justice. Despite her moral traditionalism, she's pro-redistribution, believing in structural racial inequality and the need for government intervention. She has low trust in institutions but is welcoming towards outgroups, championing immigrants' rights and LGBTQ+ causes."
